section.title: Selected research
section.intro: My recent work has focused on how tumor state influences immune recognition and response to immunotherapy, especially in breast and lung cancer models.

featured.title: CD4+ T cells and response to anti-CTLA4 therapy in quasi-mesenchymal breast tumors
featured.summary: At Cornell, I am investigating why CD73-deficient quasi-mesenchymal breast tumors, which are otherwise refractory, become sensitive to anti-CTLA4 immune checkpoint blockade. I use depletion models, complementary knockout strategies, and 14-color flow cytometry to test which immune populations drive response. This work has supported a central role for CD4+ T-helper cells in tumor regression.

cards.title: Lipid metabolism and epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity
cards.summary: In collaborative work on SCD1 and lipid desaturation, I examine how metabolic state influences epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity and, in turn, immune recognition. This project has helped me think more mechanistically about how tumor identity and therapeutic susceptibility move together.

cards.title: Spatial immune architecture in lung tumors
cards.summary: At NYU Langone, I studied tumor-associated macrophages and the spatial organization of immune cells within lung tumors. Using multiplex immunofluorescence, confocal microscopy, and image analysis, I explored how tissue architecture may relate to therapeutic response.

cards.title: Earlier training across microbial systems, public health, and computational work
cards.summary: Before focusing on cancer immunology, my work included microbial fuel cells, biomaterials, computational inhibitor screening, maternal health study coordination, and microbiome-oriented analysis. That background still shapes how I think about systems, variability, and translation.
